candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture distinguish what is truly structurally necessary from what is merely historically common? In particular: are group boundaries indispensable for large-scale coordination, trust, and norm enforcement, or could alternative mechanisms—reputation systems, institutions, markets, formal rules, algorithmic matching, radical transparency—perform the same load-bearing functions without regenerating morally insulated in-group/out-group dynamics?

What empirical signature would separate 'second-order tribalism' from legitimate moral discrimination? The conjecture risks collapsing necessary distinctions (e.g. sanctioning actual defectors, excluding harmful actors, enforcing competence) into tribalism by relabeling any durable boundary as tribe-like. A key open problem is to formulate criteria that distinguish functional norm enforcement from boundary formation driven by status, identity, and moralized coalition maintenance.

What are the system-level conditions under which suppressing explicit tribal markers causes boundary formation to become finer, more moralized, and harder to criticize, rather than weaker overall? The conjecture proposes a substitution effect, but leaves open the feedback structure: which variables—scale, anonymity, inequality, institutional trust, media environment, elite incentives, threat level, mobility—determine whether anti-tribal norms reduce conflict or instead intensify hidden prestige hierarchies and taboo-protected out-groups?
